g.data-narration: Welcome to the History of Database Applications. We will explore six decades of evolution from simple file systems to modern cloud databases.
Understanding database history helps us appreciate why modern DBMS architectures exist and their design rationale for current applications.
g.text: Database evolution spans over 60 years, driven by the need for better data
management, efficiency, and handling increasingly complex business requirements.

g.data-narration: Early file processing systems suffered from five major problems: data redundancy, program-data dependence, lack of security, no concurrent access, and integrity issues.
g.text: Limitations that drove the evolution to DBMS:
Sequential file access • Magnetic tapes • Batch processing

g.data-narration: The Hierarchical Model (1966) organized data in tree structures with parent-child relationships, pioneered by IBM's Information Management System.
g.text: IBM's IMS - Tree structure with one-to-many relationships

g.data-narration: The Network Model (1969) by CODASYL allowed many-to-many relationships through a graph structure, improving on hierarchical limitations.
g.text: CODASYL - Graph structure supporting many-to-many relationships

g.data-narration: Edgar F. Codd proposed the Relational Model in 1970, revolutionizing databases with tables, primary keys, foreign keys, and SQL based on set theory.
g.text: E.F. Codd's revolutionary paper - Mathematical foundation based on set theory
• Data independence • Declarative SQL • Set-based operations

g.data-narration: Object-Oriented databases emerged in the 1980s to handle complex objects with encapsulation, inheritance, and unique object identifiers.
g.text: Combining OOP benefits with database capabilities for complex data types

g.data-narration: NoSQL databases emerged in 2000s to handle massive unstructured data with horizontal scalability, flexible schemas, and high availability.
g.text: Not Only SQL - Addressing scalability and unstructured data challenges
Key Traits: Horizontal scalability • Flexible schema • CAP theorem trade-offs • Eventual consistency

g.data-narration: Modern trends include NewSQL combining SQL benefits with NoSQL scalability, cloud databases, and multi-model databases supporting diverse data types.
g.text: Current database landscape - Cloud, NewSQL, and Multi-model approaches

g.data-narration: In summary, database evolution progressed through file systems, hierarchical, network, relational, object-oriented, and NoSQL models to meet changing data needs.
g.text: Remember: Each evolution solved limitations of previous systems
